｢401・402・403　[[岩手県交通都南営業所#中央線|中央線]]｣、｢416　簗川線｣、｢423　孝仁病院線｣、※｢510　[[岩手県交通矢巾営業所#南インター経由川久保線|南インター経由川久保線]]｣、に乗車した場合、｢山王下｣下車。徒歩5分。 *** ただし、｢八幡宮前｣を経由する路線については、八幡宮の諸行事に伴い、経路変更する場合があるため、この場合のアクセス方法は、｢山王下｣を経由する路線に乗車した場合に準ずる[https://web.archive.org/web/20190602004312/http://www.iwatekenkotsu.co.jp/tyagutyagu-fes.html　6/8「チャグチャグ馬コ」交通規制に伴うバス運行について]　岩手県交通。 == 歴史 == [[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]）、[[源頼義]]が[[安倍氏 (奥州)|安倍氏]]討伐の際に、戦勝を祈願して[[石清水八幡宮|石清水八幡]]を[[勧請]]したのに始まると伝えられる。「鳩森八幡宮」と称し、地元の[[豪族]]の日戸氏が代々崇敬した。 [[文禄]]2年（[[1593年]]）、[[南部氏]]が[[盛岡城]]を築城した際に城内[[鎮守神|鎮守]]の神社として再建された。[[延宝]]8年（[[1680年]]）、[[南部重信]]は青森より南部氏の[[氏神]]であった[[櫛引八幡宮|櫛引八幡]]を勧請し、鳩森八幡の[[御旅所]]のあった現在地に祀って「新八幡」「南部新八幡」と称した。[[明治]]に入って鳩森八幡を新八幡に遷座、さらに[[明治]]22年（[[1889年]]）、市内の[[白山神社]]を合祀した。 [[明治]]39年（1906年）、岩手護國神社が岩手郡東中野村茶畑から当社境内に移設される。 [[平成]]14年（[[2002年]]）に正面一の[[鳥居]]が建て替えられた。旧鳥居は[[宝暦]]13年（[[1763年]]）に[[南部利雄]]（[[盛岡藩|南部藩]]8代藩主）によって築造された歴史のあるもので、境内の別の場所に移築されている。 [[2020年東京オリンピック]]の聖火リレーでセレブレーション会場となった、 聖火ランナーは公募により1万人程度が選ばれた、聖火リレーについて、 [[東京オリンピック・パラリンピック競技大会組織委員会|組織委員会]]は スポンサー企業4社と各都道府県実行委員会が行ったランナー公募に延べ 53万5717件の応募があったと発表した {{Cite news|title=聖火リレー、公募に延べ53万件応募 |newspaper=日本経済新聞|date=2019-09-27|url=https: //www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/| publication-date=|accessdate=2019年9月27日|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org /web/20190927125733/https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|archivedate=2019年9月27日}}。 == 施設・境内社 == 元々は八幡山の頂上の小祠を本殿としていたが、後にそれを囲むように本殿が建てられた。拝殿は八幡山の麓にある。 境内に笠森稲荷神社（盛岡八幡宮境内地に元々あった神社）、神明社（盛岡市神明町の名の起こりとなった神社）、梅宮（安産の神）、盛岡天神社、十二支の守り神を祀る各神社（十二支神社）、神宝殿のほか、岩手護国神社、霊璽簿奉安殿、戦没者遺骨奉安殿、岩手県[[神社庁]]がある。 == 周辺 == * 住吉神社 (盛岡市) * 盛岡天満宮 * [[大慈寺 (盛岡市)|大慈寺]] - [[原敬]]墓所 == 関連項目 == * [[チャグチャグ馬コ]] * [[米内光政]] - 昭和34年、米内光政銅像建築会により社務所前に銅像が建立される。 * [[盛岡山車]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * [http://www.morioka8man.jp 盛岡八幡宮]（公式サイト） * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061006114402/http://www.vill.takizawa.iwate.jp/ver2006/kanko/kanko_chag.html チャグチャグ馬コ]（岩手県滝沢村） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もりおかはちまんくう}} [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:盛岡市の建築物]] [[Category:盛岡市の歴史]] [[Category:岩手県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

revision.comment: Moving [[:Category:Beppo Shrines]] to [[:Category:Beppyo shrines]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2022 June 2#Category:Beppo Shrines]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月}} {{神社 |名称= 盛岡八幡宮 |画像= [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|39|41|43.5|N|141|9|50.1|E}}}} |所在地= [[岩手県]][[盛岡市]]八幡町13-1 | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 41 | 緯度秒 = 43.5 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 141 |経度分 = 9 | 経度秒 = 50.1 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |祭神= 誉田別命([[応神天皇]]、[[八幡神]])
を主祭神とし、他に
・春日大神
・白山大神
を祀る。 |社格= 旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建= [[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]） |例祭= [[9月15日]]（南部流流鏑馬神事） |神事= [[チャグチャグ馬コ]]（6月第2土曜日） |地図= Japan Iwate }} [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|一の鳥居]] [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|境内参道]] '''盛岡八幡宮'''（もりおかはちまんぐう）は、[[岩手県]][[盛岡市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。盛岡の[[総鎮守]]とされ、神事の[[チャグチャグ馬コ]]、例祭の[[山車]]行事（[[盛岡山車]]）、[[流鏑馬]]神事で知られる。 == アクセス == * [[東日本旅客鉄道|JR東日本]][[IGRいわて銀河鉄道]][[盛岡駅]]東口5番のりばより[[岩手県交通]]バス利用。※は14番のりばからの路線。 ** ｢210　[[岩手県交通滝沢営業所#青山町線|青山町線]]・茶畑(中野一丁目)行｣、｢251・252　[[岩手県交通巣子車庫#厨川中央線|厨川中央線]]・茶畑(中野一丁目)行｣、｢422　中央循環線｣、｢404・611・612　[[岩手県交通紫波営業所#長岡線|長岡線]]｣、に乗車した場合、｢八幡宮前｣下車。 ** ｢401・402・403　[[岩手県交通都南営業所#中央線|中央線]]｣、｢416　簗川線｣、｢423　孝仁病院線｣、※｢510　[[岩手県交通矢巾営業所#南インター経由川久保線|南インター経由川久保線]]｣、に乗車した場合、｢山王下｣下車。徒歩5分。 *** ただし、｢八幡宮前｣を経由する路線については、八幡宮の諸行事に伴い、経路変更する場合があるため、この場合のアクセス方法は、｢山王下｣を経由する路線に乗車した場合に準ずる[https://web.archive.org/web/20190602004312/http://www.iwatekenkotsu.co.jp/tyagutyagu-fes.html　6/8「チャグチャグ馬コ」交通規制に伴うバス運行について]　岩手県交通。 [[文禄]]2年（[[1593年]]）、[[南部氏]]が[[盛岡城]]を築城した際に城内[[鎮守神|鎮守]]の神社として再建された。[[延宝]]8年（[[1680年]]）、[[南部重信]]は青森より南部氏の[[氏神]]であった[[櫛引八幡宮|櫛引八幡]]を勧請し、鳩森八幡の[[御旅所]]のあった現在地に祀って「新八幡」「南部新八幡」と称した。[[明治]]に入って鳩森八幡を新八幡に遷座、さらに[[明治]]22年（[[1889年]]）、市内の[[白山神社]]を合祀した。 [[明治]]39年（1906年）、岩手護國神社が岩手郡東中野村茶畑から当社境内に移設される。 [[平成]]14年（[[2002年]]）に正面一の[[鳥居]]が建て替えられた。旧鳥居は[[宝暦]]13年（[[1763年]]）に[[南部利雄]]（[[盛岡藩|南部藩]]8代藩主）によって築造された歴史のあるもので、境内の別の場所に移築されている。 publication-date=|accessdate=2019年9月27日|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190927125733/https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|archivedate=2019年9月27日}}。 == 施設・境内社 == 元々は八幡山の頂上の小祠を本殿としていたが、後にそれを囲むように本殿が建てられた。拝殿は八幡山の麓にある。 境内に笠森稲荷神社（盛岡八幡宮境内地に元々あった神社）、神明社（盛岡市神明町の名の起こりとなった神社）、梅宮（安産の神）、盛岡天神社、十二支の守り神を祀る各神社（十二支神社）、神宝殿のほか、岩手護国神社、霊璽簿奉安殿、戦没者遺骨奉安殿、岩手県[[神社庁]]がある。 == 周辺 == * 住吉神社 (盛岡市) * 盛岡天満宮 * [[大慈寺 (盛岡市)|大慈寺]] - [[原敬]]墓所 == 関連項目 == * [[チャグチャグ馬コ]] * [[米内光政]] - 昭和34年、米内光政銅像建築会により社務所前に銅像が建立される。 * [[盛岡山車]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * [http://www.morioka8man.jp 盛岡八幡宮]（公式サイト） * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061006114402/http://www.vill.takizawa.iwate.jp/ver2006/kanko/kanko_chag.html チャグチャグ馬コ]（岩手県滝沢村） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もりおかはちまんくう}} [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:盛岡市の建築物]] [[Category:盛岡市の歴史]] [[Category:岩手県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]]

revision.comment: /* アクセス */ 記述の簡素化及び廃止路線の除去
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月}} {{神社 |名称= 盛岡八幡宮 |画像= [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|39|41|43.5|N|141|9|50.1|E}}}} |所在地= [[岩手県]][[盛岡市]]八幡町13-1 | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 41 | 緯度秒 = 43.5 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 141 |経度分 = 9 | 経度秒 = 50.1 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |祭神= 誉田別命([[応神天皇]]、[[八幡神]])
を主祭神とし、他に
・春日大神
・白山大神
を祀る。 |社格= 旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建= [[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]） |例祭= [[9月15日]]（南部流流鏑馬神事） |神事= [[チャグチャグ馬コ]]（6月第2土曜日） |地図= Japan Iwate }} [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|一の鳥居]] [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|境内参道]] '''盛岡八幡宮'''（もりおかはちまんぐう）は、[[岩手県]][[盛岡市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。盛岡の[[総鎮守]]とされ、神事の[[チャグチャグ馬コ]]、例祭の[[山車]]行事（[[盛岡山車]]）、[[流鏑馬]]神事で知られる。 == アクセス == * [[東日本旅客鉄道|JR東日本]][[IGRいわて銀河鉄道]][[盛岡駅]]東口5番のりばより[[岩手県交通]]バス利用。※は14番のりばからの路線。 ** ｢[[岩手県交通巣子車庫#厨川中央線|251・252]]　茶畑(中野一丁目)行｣、｢[[岩手県交通都南営業所#中央循環線|422]]・[[岩手県交通都南営業所#長岡線|404]]・[[岩手県交通紫波営業所#長岡線|611・612]]｣に乗車した場合、｢八幡宮前｣下車。 ** ｢[[岩手県交通都南営業所#中央線|401・402・403]]・[[岩手県交通都南営業所#孝仁病院線|423]]・※[[岩手県交通矢巾営業所#南インター経由川久保線|510]]｣に乗車した場合、｢山王下｣下車。徒歩5分。 *** ただし、｢八幡宮前｣を経由する路線については、八幡宮の諸行事に伴い、経路変更する場合があるため、この場合のアクセス方法は、｢山王下｣を経由する路線に乗車した場合に準ずる[https://web.archive.org/web/20190602004312/http://www.iwatekenkotsu.co.jp/tyagutyagu-fes.html　6/8「チャグチャグ馬コ」交通規制に伴うバス運行について]　岩手県交通。 == 歴史 == [[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]）、[[源頼義]]が[[安倍氏 (奥州)|安倍氏]]討伐の際に、戦勝を祈願して[[石清水八幡宮|石清水八幡]]を[[勧請]]したのに始まると伝えられる。「鳩森八幡宮」と称し、地元の[[豪族]]の日戸氏が代々崇敬した。 [[文禄]]2年（[[1593年]]）、[[南部氏]]が[[盛岡城]]を築城した際に城内[[鎮守神|鎮守]]の神社として再建された。[[延宝]]8年（[[1680年]]）、[[南部重信]]は青森より南部氏の[[氏神]]であった[[櫛引八幡宮|櫛引八幡]]を勧請し、鳩森八幡の[[御旅所]]のあった現在地に祀って「新八幡」「南部新八幡」と称した。[[明治]]に入って鳩森八幡を新八幡に遷座、さらに[[明治]]22年（[[1889年]]）、市内の[[白山神社]]を合祀した。 [[明治]]39年（1906年）、岩手護國神社が岩手郡東中野村茶畑から当社境内に移設される。 [[平成]]14年（[[2002年]]）に正面一の[[鳥居]]が建て替えられた。旧鳥居は[[宝暦]]13年（[[1763年]]）に[[南部利雄]]（[[盛岡藩|南部藩]]8代藩主）によって築造された歴史のあるもので、境内の別の場所に移築されている。 [[2020年東京オリンピック]]の聖火リレーでセレブレーション会場となった、 聖火ランナーは公募により1万人程度が選ばれた、聖火リレーについて、 [[東京オリンピック・パラリンピック競技大会組織委員会|組織委員会]]は スポンサー企業4社と各都道府県実行委員会が行ったランナー公募に延べ 53万5717件の応募があったと発表した {{Cite news|title=聖火リレー、公募に延べ53万件応募 |newspaper=日本経済新聞|date=2019-09-27|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/| publication-date=|accessdate=2019年9月27日|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190927125733/https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|archivedate=2019年9月27日}}。 == 施設・境内社 == 元々は八幡山の頂上の小祠を本殿としていたが、後にそれを囲むように本殿が建てられた。拝殿は八幡山の麓にある。 境内に笠森稲荷神社（盛岡八幡宮境内地に元々あった神社）、神明社（盛岡市神明町の名の起こりとなった神社）、梅宮（安産の神）、盛岡天神社、十二支の守り神を祀る各神社（十二支神社）、神宝殿のほか、岩手護国神社、霊璽簿奉安殿、戦没者遺骨奉安殿、岩手県[[神社庁]]がある。 == 周辺 == * 住吉神社 (盛岡市) * 盛岡天満宮 * [[大慈寺 (盛岡市)|大慈寺]] - [[原敬]]墓所 == 関連項目 == * [[チャグチャグ馬コ]] * [[米内光政]] - 昭和34年、米内光政銅像建築会により社務所前に銅像が建立される。 * [[盛岡山車]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * [http://www.morioka8man.jp 盛岡八幡宮]（公式サイト） * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061006114402/http://www.vill.takizawa.iwate.jp/ver2006/kanko/kanko_chag.html チャグチャグ馬コ]（岩手県滝沢村） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もりおかはちまんくう}} [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:盛岡市の建築物]] [[Category:盛岡市の歴史]] [[Category:岩手県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] {{Hachiman Faith}}

news|title=聖火リレー、公募に延べ53万件応募 |newspaper=日本経済新聞|date=2019-09-27|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/| publication-date=|accessdate=2019年9月27日|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190927125733/https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|archivedate=2019年9月27日}}。 == 施設・境内社 == 元々は八幡山の頂上の小祠を本殿としていたが、後にそれを囲むように本殿が建てられた。拝殿は八幡山の麓にある。 境内に笠森稲荷神社（盛岡八幡宮境内地に元々あった神社）、神明社（盛岡市神明町の名の起こりとなった神社）、梅宮（安産の神）、盛岡天神社、十二支の守り神を祀る各神社（十二支神社）、神宝殿のほか、岩手[[護国神社]]、霊璽簿奉安殿、戦没者遺骨奉安殿、岩手県[[神社庁]]がある。 == 周辺 == * 住吉神社 (盛岡市) * 盛岡天満宮 * [[大慈寺 (盛岡市)|大慈寺]] - [[原敬]]墓所 == 関連項目 == * [[チャグチャグ馬コ]] * [[米内光政]] - 昭和34年、米内光政銅像建築会により社務所前に銅像が建立される。 * [[盛岡山車]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * [http://www.morioka8man.jp 盛岡八幡宮]（公式サイト） * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061006114402/http://www.vill.takizawa.iwate.jp/ver2006/kanko/kanko_chag.html チャグチャグ馬コ]（岩手県滝沢村） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もりおかはちまんくう}} [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:盛岡市の建築物]] [[Category:盛岡市の歴史]] [[Category:岩手県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

revision.comment: 33 revisions imported from [[:wikipedia:Morioka_Hachimangū]]: Importing from English Wikipedia for the purposes of this wiki
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] {{Hachiman Faith}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月}} {{神社 |名称= 盛岡八幡宮 |画像= [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg|260px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|39|41|43.5|N|141|9|50.1|E}}}} |所在地= [[岩手県]][[盛岡市]]八幡町13-1 | 緯度度 = 39 | 緯度分 = 41 | 緯度秒 = 43.5 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 141 |経度分 = 9 | 経度秒 = 50.1 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |祭神= 誉田別命([[応神天皇]]、[[八幡神]])
を主祭神とし、他に
・春日大神
・白山大神
を祀る。 |社格= 旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建= [[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]） |例祭= [[9月15日]]（南部流流鏑馬神事） |神事= [[チャグチャグ馬コ]]（6月第2土曜日） |地図= Japan Iwate }} [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|一の鳥居]] [[ファイル:171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan06s3.jpg|thumb|境内参道]] '''盛岡八幡宮'''（もりおかはちまんぐう）は、[[岩手県]][[盛岡市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。盛岡の[[総鎮守]]とされ、神事の[[チャグチャグ馬コ]]、例祭の[[山車]]行事（[[盛岡山車]]）、[[流鏑馬]]神事で知られる。 == アクセス == * [[東日本旅客鉄道|JR東日本]][[IGRいわて銀河鉄道]][[盛岡駅]]東口5番のりばより[[岩手県交通]]バス利用。※は14番のりばからの路線。 ** ｢[[岩手県交通巣子車庫#厨川中央線|251・252]]　茶畑(中野一丁目)行｣、｢[[岩手県交通都南営業所#中央循環線|422]]・[[岩手県交通都南営業所#長岡線|404]]・[[岩手県交通紫波営業所#長岡線|611・612]]｣に乗車した場合、｢八幡宮前｣下車。 ** ｢[[岩手県交通都南営業所#中央線|401・402・403]]・[[岩手県交通都南営業所#孝仁病院線|423]]・※[[岩手県交通矢巾営業所#南インター経由川久保線|510]]｣に乗車した場合、｢山王下｣下車。徒歩5分。 *** ただし、｢八幡宮前｣を経由する路線については、八幡宮の諸行事に伴い、経路変更する場合があるため、この場合のアクセス方法は、｢山王下｣を経由する路線に乗車した場合に準ずる[https://web.archive.org/web/20190602004312/http://www.iwatekenkotsu.co.jp/tyagutyagu-fes.html　6/8「チャグチャグ馬コ」交通規制に伴うバス運行について]　岩手県交通。 == 歴史 == [[康平]]5年（[[1062年]]）、[[源頼義]]が[[安倍氏 (奥州)|安倍氏]]討伐の際に、戦勝を祈願して[[石清水八幡宮|石清水八幡]]を[[勧請]]したのに始まると伝えられる。「鳩森八幡宮」と称し、地元の[[豪族]]の日戸氏が代々崇敬した。 [[文禄]]2年（[[1593年]]）、[[南部氏]]が[[盛岡城]]を築城した際に城内[[鎮守神|鎮守]]の神社として再建された。[[延宝]]8年（[[1680年]]）、[[南部重信]]は青森より南部氏の[[氏神]]であった[[櫛引八幡宮|櫛引八幡]]を勧請し、鳩森八幡の[[御旅所]]のあった現在地に祀って「新八幡」「南部新八幡」と称した。[[明治]]に入って鳩森八幡を新八幡に遷座、さらに[[明治]]22年（[[1889年]]）、市内の[[白山神社]]を合祀した。 [[明治]]39年（1906年）、岩手護國神社が岩手郡東中野村茶畑から当社境内に移設される。 [[平成]]14年（[[2002年]]）に正面一の[[鳥居]]が建て替えられた。旧鳥居は[[宝暦]]13年（[[1763年]]）に[[南部利雄]]（[[盛岡藩|南部藩]]8代藩主）によって築造された歴史のあるもので、境内の別の場所に移築されている。 [[2020年東京オリンピック]]の聖火リレーでセレブレーション会場となった、 聖火ランナーは公募により1万人程度が選ばれた、聖火リレーについて、 [[東京オリンピック・パラリンピック競技大会組織委員会|組織委員会]]は スポンサー企業4社と各都道府県実行委員会が行ったランナー公募に延べ 53万5717件の応募があったと発表した {{Cite news|title=聖火リレー、公募に延べ53万件応募 |newspaper=日本経済新聞|date=2019-09-27|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/| publication-date=|accessdate=2019年9月27日|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190927125733/https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|archivedate=2019年9月27日}}。 == 施設・境内社 == 元々は八幡山の頂上の小祠を本殿としていたが、後にそれを囲むように本殿が建てられた。拝殿は八幡山の麓にある。 境内に笠森稲荷神社（盛岡八幡宮境内地に元々あった神社）、神明社（盛岡市神明町の名の起こりとなった神社）、梅宮（安産の神）、盛岡天神社、十二支の守り神を祀る各神社（十二支神社）、神宝殿のほか、岩手[[護国神社]]、霊璽簿奉安殿、戦没者遺骨奉安殿、岩手県[[神社庁]]がある。 == 周辺 == * 住吉神社 (盛岡市) * 盛岡天満宮 * [[大慈寺 (盛岡市)|大慈寺]] - [[原敬]]墓所 == 関連項目 == * [[チャグチャグ馬コ]] * [[米内光政]] - 昭和34年、米内光政銅像建築会により社務所前に銅像が建立される。 * [[盛岡山車]] == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * [http://www.morioka8man.jp 盛岡八幡宮]（公式サイト） * [https://web.archive.org/web/20061006114402/http://www.vill.takizawa.iwate.jp/ver2006/kanko/kanko_chag.html チャグチャグ馬コ]（岩手県滝沢村） {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もりおかはちまんくう}} [[Category:岩手県の神社]] [[Category:盛岡市の建築物]] [[Category:盛岡市の歴史]] [[Category:岩手県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine

It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Morioka Hachimangū]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] {{Hachiman Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[en:Morioka Hachimangū]]{{Hachiman Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine

It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia20]] [[Category:Dd]][[ja:盛岡八幡宮]] [[en:Morioka Hachimangū]]{{Hachiman Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[ja:盛岡八幡宮]] [[en:Morioka Hachimangū]]{{Hachiman Faith}}

Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[ja:盛岡八幡宮]] [[en:Morioka Hachimangū]]{{Hachiman Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:盛岡八幡宮]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Morioka Hachimangū]]{{Hachiman Faith}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category|Morioka-Hachimangū}} * {{official website|http://www.morioka8man.jp/ }} {{in lang|ja}} ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Morioka Hachimangu}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:1060s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:1062 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Morioka, Iwate]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1062]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:11th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:盛岡八幡宮]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dardar]] [[en:Morioka Hachimangū]]{{Hachiman Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Morioka Hachiman Shrine
盛岡八幡宮 | image = 171104 Morioka-Hachimangu Morioka Iwate pref Japan04s3.jpg | caption = Morioka Hachimangu Honden | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|39|41|43.11|N|141|09|50.62|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman Shrine]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 1062 | date_destroyed = | location = Yawata-cho 13-2, Morioka, Iwate | website = {{URL|http://www.morioka8man.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Hachiman-zukuri]] | festival = September 15 | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Morioka Hachimangū'''|盛岡八幡宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Morioka, Iwate]] in northern [[Japan]]. The shrine is noted for its [[matsuri|annual festival]] on the second Saturday in June, which is famous for the ''[[Chagu Chagu Umakko]]'', a horse parade which was recognized in 1978 as an [[Intangible Folk Cultural Property]].{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/312/198 |title=Database of National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=18 April 2011}} In 1996 the sound of the bells of the ''Chagu Chagu Umakko'' was selected by the [[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] as one of the [[100 Soundscapes of Japan]].{{cite web |url=http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |title=北海道・東北 - チャグチャグ馬コの鈴の音 |publisher=[[Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|Ministry of the Environment]] |accessdate=18 April 2011 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120608002719/http://www.env.go.jp/air/life/oto/ |archivedate=8 June 2012 }} The shrine is also noted for its displays of ''[[yabusame]]'' horse archery during its annual festival on September 15. ==History== The Morioka Hachimangū was established in 1062 during the late [[Heian period]] when [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] brought a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the [[Minamoto clan]]’s tutelary shrine, the [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] in Kyoto to pray for victory in his campaign against the [[Abe clan]] in the [[Former Nine Years War]]. It was originally called the {{nihongo|Hatomori Hachimangū|鳩森八幡宮||}}. The shrine was rebuilt in 1593 by the [[Nanbu clan]] to be the protective shrine for [[Morioka Castle]]. Under the [[State Shinto]] system of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|shrine ranking]] from 1871 through 1946, the Morioka Hachimangū was officially designated as a "prefectural shrine". The Edo-period shrine structures burned down in 1884. The present main structure dates from 2006. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' [[Hachiman]]. Hachiman has been recognized as an amalgamation of the semi-legendary [[Emperor Ojin]] and his consort, [[Empress Jingū]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{OCLC|43487317}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC| 399449}} ==External links== {{commons
